title: Operators as Conservation Laws
subtitle: Write-Time Constraints from Event Primitives — Why ATRANS Conserves and MTRANS Doesn't

children.children: Here's a question nobody asked about Schank's Conceptual Dependency theory: why does ATRANS (transfer of possession) look different from MTRANS (transfer of information)? Both involve something moving from A to B. But ATRANS is a zero-sum game — John loses the book, Mary gains the book, total books unchanged. MTRANS is not — John tells Mary a secret, but John still knows it. The total amount of 'knowing' increases.

This isn't a quirk of Schank's notation. It's a conservation law. And it turns out you can derive the entire operator taxonomy — the one our wake-sleep system rediscovered from compression — purely from which quantities are conserved and which aren't.

props.source: In physics, conservation of energy says you can't create or destroy energy — only transfer it between systems. A ball rolls downhill: potential energy converts to kinetic energy, but the total stays constant.
props.target: ATRANS is conservation of possession. If Mary gains a book, someone must lose a book. MTRANS violates this 'conservation law' — information can be copied without the source losing anything. This structural difference is what makes them fundamentally different operators.

children.children: Core Claim: Schankian operators are conservation laws over entity-property pairs. The operator taxonomy can be derived purely from the algebraic structure of state diffs — which quantities are preserved, created, or destroyed — without any semantic labels.

For any operator, we can compute a conservation matrix. Consider the state diff — the set of triples added and removed. For each property type (has, location, knows, feels, etc.), count the net change across all entities:
State diff: −(John, has, book) +(Mary, has, book)
Conservation check for 'has': Removed 1 instance, Added 1 instance → Net change = 0 → CONSERVED ✓
This holds for every ATRANS event: give, sell, donate, steal. The 'has' property total never changes. Something always moves from one entity to another.
State diff: +(Mary, knows, secret) — note: nothing removed from John!
Conservation check for 'knows': Removed 0, Added 1 → Net change = +1 → NOT CONSERVED ✗
Information is duplicated, not transferred. This is why MTRANS uses the SET operator (add only) while ATRANS uses MOVE_PROP (remove + add). The conservation structure demands different primitives.
State diff: −(John, location, home) +(John, location, park)
Conservation check for 'location': Removed 1 instance, Added 1 instance → Net change = 0 → CONSERVED ✓ (with a twist: same entity changes value, so it's a CHANGE, not a MOVE_PROP)
State diff: −(John, has, apple) +(John, consumed, apple)

Conservation check for 'has': Removed 1, Added 0 → Net change = −1 → DESTROYED. But 'consumed' increases by 1 → CREATED. INGEST converts one property type into another — like matter converting to energy.

Now here's the punchline. If you compute the conservation matrix for every discovered operator and cluster them by their conservation signatures, you get exactly Schank's taxonomy — without ever looking at the semantic labels:
Transfer Operators (conservation ≥ 0 across all properties)
ATRANS (MOVE_PROP_has): possession conserved. PTRANS (CHANGE_location): location conserved (value changes, count doesn't). These are the 'physics-like' operators — zero-sum games.
Creation Operators (net positive on at least one property)
MTRANS (SET_knows): information created (copied without loss). SET_feels: emotional state created. SET_consumed: consumption state created. These break conservation — they add to the world.
Destruction Operators (net negative on at least one property)
UNSET_has: possession destroyed (object lost, no recipient). These remove from the world without compensating addition.
Transformation Operators (property type conversion)
INGEST: converts 'has' into 'consumed'. CHANGE_feels: converts one emotional state into another. Total 'stuff' may be conserved, but its type changes — like nuclear transmutation.

Note: Our wake-sleep system discovers this taxonomy from compression pressure alone. It doesn't know about conservation laws. But the operators it finds happen to cluster perfectly by conservation signature — because conservation structure IS compression structure. Operators that conserve totals need fewer bits to describe (the 'removed' set predicts the 'added' set).

Here's where the insight becomes practical. If operators are conservation laws, then they imply constraints that can be enforced at write time in a knowledge graph. When you assert an ATRANS event, the system can automatically:
Precondition checking
ATRANS: verify the source actually possesses the object. 'Mary gave John the book' fails if Mary doesn't have the book. The conservation law demands a source.
Consistency maintenance
PTRANS: automatically invalidate the old location. If John moves to the park, he can't also be at home. Conservation of location occupancy = mutual exclusion.
Conservation exceptions

MTRANS: do NOT invalidate the source's knowledge. John telling Mary the secret doesn't mean John forgets it. This constraint is derivable — not hand-coded — from the operator's conservation signature.

This connects directly to the WorldDB paper (arXiv:2604.18478), which treats edges as 'write-time programs' with on_insert/on_delete handlers. Our insight: these handlers aren't arbitrary — they're determined by the operator's conservation class. You don't need to code them per-domain; you derive them from the operator taxonomy.

In physics, Emmy Noether proved that every conservation law corresponds to a symmetry. Conservation of energy ↔ time-translation symmetry. Conservation of momentum ↔ space-translation symmetry. Can we find the 'symmetries' behind our operator conservation laws?

ATRANS conserves possession. The corresponding 'symmetry' is: the system is invariant under relabeling of possessors. It doesn't matter WHO has the book — the total count of 'has' relations stays the same. The operator only moves ownership; it doesn't create or destroy it.
MTRANS breaks this symmetry for information. Knowledge is NOT invariant under relabeling — telling someone something creates new knowledge. Information has different 'physics' than matter.

This maps to a deep distinction in physics: matter obeys conservation laws (you can't create or destroy atoms in chemistry, only rearrange). Information obeys different laws (you CAN copy a bit; quantum no-cloning is the exception, not the rule). Schank's ATRANS/MTRANS distinction captures exactly this physical/informational divide.

props.source: In thermodynamics, processes are classified by what they conserve: isothermal (temperature), isobaric (pressure), isochoric (volume), adiabatic (heat). Each conservation type defines a different class of physical process.
props.target: In event semantics, operators are classified by what they conserve: ATRANS (possession count), PTRANS (location uniqueness), MTRANS (nothing — information is free). Each conservation type defines a different class of event.

children.children: Try classifying these events by their conservation signature:

props.title: Classify the Conservation Type
props.description: For each event, determine: does the affected property's total count increase, decrease, or stay the same?

steps.label: Event: 'Alice sold Bob the car'
steps.detail: State diff: −(Alice, has, car) +(Bob, has, car). Property 'has': removed 1, added 1 → Net = 0 → CONSERVED. This is ATRANS.

steps.label: Event: 'The teacher taught the student calculus'
steps.detail: State diff: +(student, knows, calculus). Property 'knows': removed 0, added 1 → Net = +1 → CREATED. This is MTRANS (SET_knows). The teacher still knows calculus!

steps.label: Event: 'The cat walked from the kitchen to the garden'
steps.detail: State diff: −(cat, location, kitchen) +(cat, location, garden). Property 'location': removed 1, added 1 → Net = 0 → CONSERVED. This is PTRANS (CHANGE_location). A cat can only be in one place.

steps.label: Event: 'She drank the coffee'
steps.detail: State diff: −(she, has, coffee) +(she, consumed, coffee). Property 'has': Net = −1 (DESTROYED). Property 'consumed': Net = +1 (CREATED). This is INGEST — a property-type conversion. The coffee doesn't vanish; it changes category.

steps.label: Event: 'The news made him sad'
steps.detail: State diff: +(him, feels, sad). Property 'feels': Net = +1 → CREATED. This is CHANGE_feels (actually SET_feels) — an emotional state is created. No corresponding emotion is 'used up'. Schank had no operator for this!

children.children: Here's the complete conservation classification of our discovered operators, organized by their algebraic structure:

props.title: Conservation Signatures of Discovered Operators
props.description: Each operator's signature tells you what happens to property totals when the event occurs.

steps.label: MOVE_PROP_has (≈ ATRANS): [has: 0, other: 0]
steps.detail: Conserves 'has' count. Zero-sum transfer: source loses exactly what destination gains. The 'purest' conservation law in the system. Usage: give, sell, donate, steal.

steps.label: CHANGE_location (≈ PTRANS): [location: 0]
steps.detail: Conserves location count (always exactly 1 per entity). Changes the VALUE but not the COUNT. Single-entity conservation. Usage: walk, drive, fly.

steps.label: SET_knows (≈ MTRANS): [knows: +1]
steps.detail: CREATES knowledge. Does not conserve — the total amount of 'knowing' in the world increases. This is the fundamental asymmetry between information and matter. Usage: tell, teach, inform.

steps.label: SET_consumed (≈ INGEST): [has: −1, consumed: +1]
steps.detail: DESTROYS possession, CREATES consumption. Type conversion — total 'stuff' is conserved but changes category. Usage: eat, drink.

steps.label: CHANGE_feels (novel): [feels: 0 or +1]
steps.detail: If replacing an emotion: conserved (sad → happy, net 0). If adding first emotion: created (+1). Context-dependent conservation. Usage: cheer, anger, comfort.

steps.label: CHANGE_wants (novel, ATOMIC top operator at 20%): [wants: 0 or +1]
steps.detail: Same pattern as CHANGE_feels — context-dependent conservation. The fact that this is the #1 ATOMIC operator suggests that human commonsense reasoning is mostly about tracking DESIRES, not physical actions. Schank completely missed this.

children.children: There's a beautiful information-theoretic explanation for why our MDL-based operator discovery naturally finds conservation-respecting operators.

When a property is conserved (removed count = added count), the 'added' set is partially predicted by the 'removed' set. If John loses a book, SOMEONE must gain a book. This predictability is redundancy, and redundancy is exactly what compression exploits.

A conserved-transfer operator (ATRANS) needs fewer bits to encode than two independent SET/UNSET operations because the second half is constrained by the first. MDL naturally prefers the conserved operator — not because it 'knows about' conservation, but because conservation IS compressibility.

Conversely, MTRANS (non-conserving) can't be compressed into a transfer — there IS no removed set to predict from. So it stays as a separate SET operator. The conservation taxonomy emerges from compression because conservation and compression are two perspectives on the same mathematical structure: mutual information between parts of the state diff.

Conservation laws, compression, and operator taxonomy are three views of the same thing. Conservation creates redundancy. Redundancy enables compression. Compression discovers operators. The operators respect conservation — completing the circle.

props.question: Why does MTRANS (information transfer) use a different base operator than ATRANS (possession transfer)?
options: Because Schank arbitrarily defined them differently
Because information doesn't obey conservation — the source retains what the destination gains
Because information is less important than physical objects
Because they operate on different entity types
props.explanation: The key structural difference is conservation. ATRANS is zero-sum (source loses what destination gains), so it uses MOVE_PROP. MTRANS is non-conserving (source retains), so it uses SET. This isn't arbitrary — it's forced by the algebraic structure of the state diff.

props.question: What is the conservation signature of 'John traded his book for Mary's pen'?
props.explanation: Trade is ATRANS ∘ ATRANS — two conservation-preserving transfers composed. John loses a book (+0 net) but gains a pen. Mary loses a pen but gains a book. Total 'has' count: unchanged. Conservation composes: the composition of conserving operators is conserving.

props.question: Why does MDL-based compression naturally discover conservation-respecting operators?
options: The algorithm is explicitly programmed to look for conservation
Conservation creates redundancy (removed set predicts added set), and compression exploits redundancy
Conservation laws are built into the seed operators
It's a coincidence — conservation has nothing to do with compression
props.explanation: Conservation means the 'removed' part of a state diff constrains the 'added' part. This mutual information is redundancy. MDL compression exploits exactly this: a single MOVE_PROP operator needs fewer bits than two independent SET/UNSET operations because the second half is predictable from the first. Conservation IS compressibility.

children.children: This conservation-law perspective opens several research directions:
Knowledge graph integrity
Derive write-time constraints from operator conservation signatures. An ATRANS assertion auto-checks source possession; a PTRANS auto-invalidates old location. No per-domain rule authoring needed.
Anomaly detection

Events that violate their expected conservation signature are anomalous. 'John gave Mary the book but John still has the book' violates ATRANS conservation. This gives a principled definition of narrative inconsistency.
Cross-domain transfer

Conservation signatures are domain-independent. Financial transactions (ATRANS of money), logistics (PTRANS of goods), education (MTRANS of knowledge) all share the same conservation structure. Learn once, apply everywhere.
Quantum information analogy

In quantum mechanics, the no-cloning theorem says you CAN'T copy quantum states — unlike classical information. MTRANS (classical copying) has no quantum analogue. This suggests a deeper connection between Schankian operators and information-theoretic limits.

Today's WorldDB paper (arXiv:2604.18478) validates this direction: their write-time reconciliation programs (supersession, contradiction, merge) are exactly the kind of constraints that conservation laws predict. The difference: they hand-code the handlers; we can derive them from the operator taxonomy.
